step_by_step_thinking:
The patient's symptoms of progressive jaundice, pruritus, and clay-colored stools suggest obstruction of the bile flow. Imaging reveals dilation of the pancreatic and common bile ducts, pointing towards a blockage near the ampulla of Vater where both the pancreatic and common bile ducts converge. Given the history of chronic smoking, which increases the risk for pancreatic adenocarcinoma, along with the characteristic obstructive pattern seen on imaging, the most likely diagnosis would be periampullary carcinoma involving either the duodenum or the distal common bile duct/pancreatic duct junction.
\boxed{B} Based on the clinical picture and imaging findings consistent with an obstructed biliary pathway near the ampulla of Vater, coupled with the high-risk factor of chronic smoking, the most probable diagnosis aligns best with periampullary carcinoma.
